section.title: The Prophecy against Gog
section.sectionRationale: After the promise of a new heart, a new spirit, and a nation raised from the dry bones of exile, one question lingers: could a fresh invasion simply undo Israel's restored peace? This vision answers with a resounding no. Gog of Magog, leading a vast coalition, rises against a peaceful, resettled land, and the LORD Himself intervenes to shatter him completely.
section.thesis: However fierce and coordinated the last assault of hostile nations may be, the LORD will intervene to break it entirely, vindicating His holiness before the watching world and confirming that His restored people will never again be left defenseless.
section.frameNote: Five scenes: the summons of Gog and his allies; Gog's evil scheme against a peaceful land; the LORD's furious intervention; Gog's total defeat and burial; and the feast of judgment giving way to Israel's lasting restoration.

units.label: The summons against Gog
units.note: a vast coalition from the far north

verses.text: And the word of the LORD came to me, saying,
“Son of man, set your face against Gog of the land of Magog, the chief prince of Meshech and Tubal. Prophesy against him
and declare that this is what the Lord GOD says: Behold, I am against you, O Gog, chief prince of Meshech and Tubal.

I will turn you around, put hooks in your jaws, and bring you out with all your army, your horses, your horsemen in full armor, and a great company armed with shields and bucklers, all brandishing their swords.
Persia, Cush, and Put will accompany them, all with shields and helmets,
as well as Gomer with all its troops, and Beth-togarmah from the far north with all its troops, the many nations with you.
Get ready; prepare yourself, you and all your company gathered around you; you will be their guard.

After a long time you will be summoned. In the latter years you will enter a land that has recovered from war, whose people were gathered from many nations to the mountains of Israel, which had long been desolate. They had been brought out from the nations, and all now dwell {{batach|securely}}.
You and all your troops, and many peoples with you will go up, advancing like a thunderstorm; you will be like a cloud covering the land.

units.label: Gog's evil scheme
units.note: an unprovoked assault on a peaceful land

verses.text: This is what the Lord GOD says: On that day, thoughts will arise in your mind, and you will devise an evil plan.
You will say, ‘I will go up against a land of unwalled villages; I will come against a tranquil people who dwell securely, all of them living without walls or bars or gates,

in order to seize the spoil and carry off the plunder, to turn a hand against the desolate places now inhabited and against a people gathered from the nations, who have acquired livestock and possessions and who live at the center of the land.’

Sheba and Dedan and the merchants of Tarshish with all its villages will ask, ‘Have you come to capture the plunder? Have you assembled your hordes to carry away loot, to make off with silver and gold, to take cattle and goods, to seize great spoil?’
Therefore prophesy, son of man, and tell Gog that this is what the Lord GOD says: On that day when My people Israel are dwelling securely, will you not take notice of this?
And you will come from your place out of the far north, you and many peoples with you, all riding horses, a mighty horde, a huge army.

You will advance against My people Israel like a cloud covering the land. It will happen in the latter days, O Gog, that I will bring you against My land, so that the nations may know Me when I show Myself holy in you before their eyes.

units.label: The LORD's furious intervention
units.note: earthquake, plague, and fire from heaven

verses.text: This is what the Lord GOD says: Are you the one of whom I have spoken in former days through My servants, the prophets of Israel, who in those times prophesied for years that I would bring you against them?
Now on that day when Gog comes against the land of Israel, declares the Lord GOD, My wrath will flare up.
In My zeal and fiery rage I proclaim that on that day there will be a great earthquake in the land of Israel.

The fish of the sea, the birds of the air, the beasts of the field, every creature that crawls upon the ground, and all mankind on the face of the earth will tremble at My presence. The mountains will be thrown down, the cliffs will collapse, and every wall will fall to the ground.
And I will summon a sword against Gog on all My mountains, declares the Lord GOD, and every man’s sword will be against his brother.
I will execute judgment upon him with plague and bloodshed. I will pour out torrents of rain, hailstones, fire, and sulfur on him and on his troops and on the many nations with him.
I will magnify and {{qadash|sanctify}} Myself, and will reveal Myself in the sight of many nations. Then they will know that I am the LORD.
“As for you, O son of man, prophesy against Gog and declare that this is what the Lord GOD says: Behold, I am against you, O Gog, chief prince of Meshech and Tubal.
I will turn you around, drive you along, bring you up from the far north, and send you against the mountains of Israel.
Then I will strike the bow from your left hand and dash down the arrows from your right hand.
On the mountains of Israel you will fall, you and all your troops and the nations with you. I will give you as food to every kind of ravenous bird and wild beast.
You will fall in the open field, for I have spoken, declares the Lord GOD.
I will send fire on Magog and on those who dwell securely in the coastlands, and they will know that I am the LORD.
So I will make My holy name known among My people Israel and will no longer allow it to be profaned. Then the nations will know that I am the LORD, the Holy One in Israel.
Yes, it is coming, and it will surely happen, declares the Lord GOD. This is the day of which I have spoken.

Then those who dwell in the cities of Israel will go out, kindle fires, and burn up the weapons, the bucklers and shields, the bows and arrows, the clubs and spears. For seven years they will use them for fuel.

They will not gather wood from the countryside or cut it from the forests, for they will use the weapons for fuel. They will loot those who looted them and plunder those who plundered them, declares the Lord GOD.

And on that day I will give Gog a burial place in Israel, the Valley of the Travelers, east of the Sea. It will block those who travel through, because Gog and all his hordes will be buried there. So it will be called the Valley of Hamon-gog.
For seven months the house of Israel will be burying them in order to cleanse the land.
All the people of the land will bury them, and it will bring them renown on the day I display My {{kavod|glory}}, declares the Lord GOD.
And men will be employed to continually pass through the land to cleanse it by burying the invaders who remain on the ground. At the end of the seven months they will begin their search.
As they pass through the land, anyone who sees a human bone will set up a pillar next to it, until the gravediggers have buried it in the Valley of Hamon-gog.
(Even the city will be named Hamonah.) And so they will cleanse the land.

units.label: The feast of judgment and Israel restored

verses.text: And as for you, son of man, this is what the Lord GOD says: Call out to every kind of bird and to every beast of the field: ‘Assemble and come together from all around to the sacrificial feast that I am preparing for you, a great feast on the mountains of Israel. There you will eat flesh and drink blood.
You will eat the flesh of the mighty and drink the blood of the princes of the earth as though they were rams, lambs, goats, and bulls, all the fattened animals of Bashan.
At the sacrifice I am preparing, you will eat fat until you are gorged and drink blood until you are drunk.
And at My table you will eat your fill of horses and riders, of mighty men and warriors of every kind,’ declares the Lord GOD.
I will display My glory among the nations, and all the nations will see the judgment that I execute and the hand that I lay upon them.
From that day forward the house of Israel will know that I am the LORD their God.

And the nations will know that the house of Israel went into exile for their iniquity, because they were unfaithful to Me. So I hid My face from them and delivered them into the hands of their enemies, so that they all fell by the sword.
I dealt with them according to their uncleanness and transgressions, and I hid My face from them.
Therefore this is what the Lord GOD says: Now I will restore Jacob from captivity and will have compassion on the whole house of Israel, and I will be jealous for My holy name.
They will forget their disgrace and all the treachery they committed against Me, when they dwell securely in their land, with no one to frighten them.
When I bring them back from the peoples and gather them out of the lands of their enemies, I will show My holiness in them in the sight of many nations.
Then they will know that I am the LORD their God, when I regather them to their own land, not leaving any of them behind after their exile among the nations.
And I will no longer hide My face from them, for I will pour out My {{ruach|Spirit}} on the house of Israel, declares the Lord GOD.”

batach.gloss: To trust, to feel secure, to dwell without fear. The word recurs like a refrain through this vision, describing Israel's peace before the invasion and, again, the LORD's promise of lasting security after Gog's defeat, 39:26.

qadash.gloss: To be set apart, holy, sanctified. God says He will sanctify Himself in the sight of the nations; His holiness is put beyond doubt not by argument but by decisive action in history.

kavod.gloss: Glory, weight, the visible splendor of God's presence. The same word describes the throne vision of chapter 1; here God says even the defeat and burial of Gog will display His glory before the watching nations.

ruach.gloss: Spirit, wind, breath. The vision closes not on the battlefield but with this word: the LORD pouring out His own Spirit on His people, the ultimate sign of restored, unbroken fellowship.

basic.context: This vision comes after the promises of a new heart, a new spirit, and a nation raised from the dry bones of exile (chapters 36-37). One question remains: if Israel is restored and finally at peace, could a fresh invasion simply undo it all? Gog of Magog, leading a vast coalition from 'the far north,' rises against the resettled, unsuspecting land. But this is not a story of Israel's vulnerability; it is a story of the LORD's intervention. He Himself draws Gog out and judges him with earthquake, plague, and fire from heaven, defeating and burying him so completely that even the birds and beasts feast on the invaders. Then, unexpectedly, the vision closes in tenderness, with God's compassion restored and His Spirit poured out.
basic.hermeneutics: This is apocalyptic prophecy, written to give unshakable assurance rather than a political timetable. The vivid, almost excessive detail, seven years of fuel from captured weapons, seven months of burial, birds summoned to a grotesque feast, portrays the totality and certainty of God's victory rather than supplying a calendar or a map. Readers should resist pinning Gog, Magog, Meshech, and Tubal onto any specific modern nation; the church has read this passage with a range of views across the centuries, and Revelation itself reuses the names for every hostile power arrayed against God at history's end.
basic.originalLanguages: Israel's peace is described with batach, to dwell securely, trusting and unafraid, both before Gog's invasion and after his defeat. God says He will sanctify Himself, qadash, before the nations, and that the whole episode will display His kavod, glory, the same word used of His throne in chapter 1. And the vision ends with ruach, Spirit, poured out on His restored people, the last and gentlest word in a chapter full of thunder.
basic.typology: Gog's massed, hopeless assault against God's resettled and undefended people typifies every last rebellion against God's reign. Revelation 20:7-10 draws directly on this vision, naming a final coalition 'Gog and Magog,' gathered from the four corners of the earth for one last battle that ends, just as here, in fire from heaven and total defeat.
basic.christ: The New Testament reads this scene as a preview of the judgment Christ will execute at His return. Paul describes Christ disarming every hostile power at the cross, Colossians 2:15, and coming again to destroy every enemy, 1 Corinthians 15:24-26, while Revelation pictures Him leading heaven's armies to the very supper of birds this chapter anticipates, Revelation 19:17-21. The God who buries Gog and pours out His Spirit on Israel is the same God who, in Christ, secures His people forever.
basic.god: God is sovereign even over enemies who imagine themselves free agents; He is the one who draws Gog out (38:4) even as Gog schemes on his own initiative. He is holy, unwilling to let His name be profaned further, and yet He is deeply compassionate, closing this fearsome vision not with more war but with restored fellowship and His Spirit poured out on His people.
basic.crossReferences: Revelation 20:7-10 borrows the very names Gog and Magog for the final, doomed rebellion against God's people. Revelation 19:17-21 pictures the same grim supper of birds feasting on defeated armies. Romans 8:31-39 promises that no hostile power can separate God's people from His love.
basic.oneStory: From Eden's serpent to Gog's massed armies to Revelation's final battle, the Bible tells one story: every power raised against God and His people, however vast or coordinated, is already defeated. The LORD who buries Gog and cleanses the land is the same LORD who, in Christ, has crushed the ultimate enemy and will one day banish evil from His creation entirely, so that His people may dwell securely with Him forever.

context.literary: The climax of the restoration section that began in chapter 33, following the promise of new life in chapters 36-37 with a final apocalyptic test: can anything now undo Israel's restored peace? The vision answers in the vivid, repetitive style typical of apocalyptic literature, piling up detail to underline the totality of God's victory.
context.historical: Addressed to exiles who had every reason to fear that any future peace would prove temporary, this vision insists that when God restores His people, that peace cannot be undone by even the mightiest coalition of the nations, named here, somewhat mysteriously, as Gog of Magog with allies gathered from every horizon.
context.canonical: This passage supplies the names Gog and Magog reused in Revelation 20 for the final rebellion at the end of the age, making Ezekiel 38-39 one of Scripture's most influential apocalyptic templates.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To assure a resettled but still vulnerable-feeling Israel that no future coalition of hostile nations can overturn the peace God has given them, and that God Himself will act decisively, publicly, and completely to defend His name and His people.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The vision describes, in apocalyptic imagery, God's certain future victory; it does not prescribe a specific timetable, geography, or modern political identification. The enduring truth is theological: hostile power, however massed, cannot prevail against God's purposes for His people.

debates.question: Who or what is Gog of Magog?

views.label: A specific historical or future coalition
views.case: Some read Meshech, Tubal, Gomer, and Persia as identifiable ancient peoples north and east of Israel, and look for a corresponding coalition to arise in the future, whether in antiquity or in a still-future crisis.

views.label: A symbolic name for every end-time hostile power
views.case: Others read Gog and Magog as apocalyptic shorthand for the sum of hostile nations in any age, not tied to a specific people; Revelation 20:8 supports this by reusing the names for a coalition drawn from 'the four corners of the earth,' clearly not a single geographic bloc.

debates.bearing: Interpretive humility is warranted here; the text itself resists confident modern geopolitical identification. The pastoral center holds regardless of view: no coalition of evil, however described, can overturn God's purposes for His people.

debates.question: What does the feast of birds and beasts (39:17-20) picture?

views.label: The grotesque completeness of Gog's defeat
views.case: The invaders who came to seize plunder and livestock (38:12-13) become themselves the plunder, feeding scavengers rather than receiving worship, a scene of horror underscoring how total the reversal is.

views.label: A direct type of the final judgment in Revelation 19
views.case: Revelation 19:17-21 pictures an angel summoning birds to 'the great supper of God' to eat the flesh of defeated kings and armies, closely echoing this scene and applying it explicitly to Christ's final victory.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree this is not contradicted by, but rather fulfilled in, the New Testament's picture of Christ's return; the imagery reinforces the certainty of final judgment on every power opposed to God and His people.

study.originalLanguages: Israel's peace is described with batach, to dwell securely, trusting and unafraid, both before Gog's invasion and after his defeat in 39:26. God says twice over that He will sanctify Himself, qadash, before the nations, His holiness proven not by argument but by action. The chapter insists the whole episode displays His kavod, glory, the same word used of the throne vision in chapter 1. And it ends, startlingly, with ruach, Spirit, poured out on the restored house of Israel, the gentlest word in a vision full of thunder, plague, and fire.

typology.type: Gog's massed, final assault against a peaceful, resettled people
typology.fulfillment: Directly echoed in Revelation 20:7-10, where a coalition called 'Gog and Magog' is gathered from the four corners of the earth for one last rebellion that God ends instantly with fire from heaven.

typology.type: The feast of birds and beasts on the defeated armies, 39:17-20
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the 'great supper of God' in Revelation 19:17-21, where the same imagery marks the final, public defeat of every power opposed to Christ.

study.christ: The New Testament reads this vision as a preview of the judgment Christ will execute at His return. Paul describes Christ disarming every hostile power at the cross, Colossians 2:15, and coming again to destroy every last enemy, 1 Corinthians 15:24-26, while Revelation pictures Him leading heaven's armies to the very supper of birds this chapter anticipates, Revelation 19:17-21. The God who buries Gog and then pours out His Spirit on Israel is the same God who, in Christ, has already secured His people and will finally banish every hostile power from His new creation.
study.god: God is sovereign even over enemies who imagine themselves free agents; He is the one who draws Gog out (38:4) even as Gog devises his own evil scheme. He is holy and jealous for His name, unwilling to let it be profaned further, and yet He is deeply compassionate, closing this fearsome vision not with more war but with restored fellowship, forgotten disgrace, and His Spirit poured out on His people.

crossReferences.note: John borrows the names Gog and Magog for the final, doomed rebellion against God's people before the last judgment.
The 'great supper of God,' where birds are summoned to eat the flesh of defeated kings and armies, directly echoes Ezekiel 39:17-20.
No coalition of hostile power, however vast, can separate God's people from His love or overturn His purpose for them.
Another prophetic vision of nations gathered for judgment before the LORD's decisive intervention.

study.oneStory: From Eden's serpent to Gog's massed armies to Revelation's final battle, the Bible tells one story: every power raised against God and His people, however vast or coordinated, is already defeated. The LORD who buries Gog and cleanses the land is the same LORD who, in Christ, has crushed the ultimate enemy and will one day banish evil from His creation entirely, so that His people may dwell securely with Him forever.

quiz.q: Who does the LORD raise up against Israel's resettled land, and from where does he come?

options.text: Gog of Magog, but coming from the south with Egypt's armies
options.note: The person is right, Gog of Magog, but his origin is the far north, not the south.

options.text: Gog, chief prince of Meshech and Tubal, from the far north
options.note: Ezekiel 38:2 and 38:15 name Gog and place him coming from the uttermost parts of the north.

options.text: An unnamed enemy whose origin is never given
options.note: The text is specific: Gog of Magog, chief prince of Meshech and Tubal.

options.text: Pharaoh, king of Egypt, from the south
options.note: Egypt is addressed elsewhere in Ezekiel, but this vision names Gog of Magog from the north.

quiz.q: According to 38:10-13, what is Gog's evil scheme?

options.text: To rebuild the ruined cities of Israel as an ally
options.note: Gog notices the rebuilt land, but his intent is to plunder it, not help build it.

options.text: To flee before Israel's approaching army
options.note: Gog advances to attack, not to flee.

options.text: To plunder a peaceful, unwalled, newly resettled and prospering land
options.note: 38:11-12 describes exactly this plan to seize spoil from an undefended people.

options.text: To make a lasting peace covenant with Israel
options.note: His stated aim is conquest and plunder, not peace.

quiz.q: How does the LORD respond when Gog invades the land?

options.text: By sending an angel to negotiate surrender terms
options.note: No negotiation is described; the LORD Himself intervenes directly.

options.text: With a great earthquake, plague, torrential rain, hailstones, fire, and sulfur, judging Gog and vindicating His holiness
options.note: 38:19-23 describes this full, direct divine judgment.

options.text: By causing Gog's own troops to retreat without a fight
options.note: There is a battle described, including troops turning on each other, but it is far more than a bloodless retreat.

options.text: He remains silent and lets the invasion proceed unchecked
options.note: The opposite happens; God intervenes decisively.

quiz.q: What happens to the weapons and to Gog's dead after the defeat?

options.text: The weapons and bodies disappear instantly without any human involvement
options.note: Israel's own people do the burning and burying over years and months.

options.text: Israel keeps the weapons as trophies but leaves the bodies where they fell
options.note: The weapons are burned for fuel, and the bodies are carefully buried to cleanse the land, not left.

options.text: Israel burns the weapons for fuel for seven years and spends seven months burying the dead to cleanse the land
options.note: 39:9-14 describes both the seven years of fuel and the seven months of burial.

options.text: Israel returns the captured weapons to the surviving nations
options.note: The weapons are burned, not returned.

quiz.q: What does the LORD promise His people after Gog's defeat, in 39:25-29?

options.text: That He will rebuild the temple but withhold His own presence from it
options.note: The promise is the opposite: restored presence, not absence, culminating in the Spirit poured out.

options.text: That He will scatter Israel among the nations once again
options.note: The promise is regathering and restoration, not further scattering.

options.text: That He will restore Jacob, have compassion on the whole house of Israel, and pour out His Spirit, hiding His face no longer
options.note: 39:25-29 gives exactly this promise of restored compassion and the Spirit poured out.

options.text: That Israel will remain safe only as long as no enemy ever threatens again
options.note: The promise is unconditional restoration and God's abiding presence, not a fragile peace dependent on circumstances.

questions: Gog's plan targets a people who feel safe and undefended (38:11). Where in your own life does comfort or security tempt you to forget how closely God still watches over you?

The LORD says He acts 'so that the nations may know Me' (38:16). How does knowing that even judgment ultimately serves God's self-revealing purpose change the way you pray about evil and injustice in the world?

This vision speaks in apocalyptic assurance rather than handing readers a political map. Where are you tempted to trade patient trust in God's promised victory for confident speculation about current events?

The chapter ends not with the battlefield but with restoration and the Spirit poured out (39:25-29). How does knowing that God's last word over His people is compassion, not war, shape your hope for the future?
Revelation later borrows the names Gog and Magog for every power that opposes God at history's end. How does the certainty of that final defeat strengthen you to stand firm today?

translationNotes.note: The Hebrew behind 'chief prince' can be read two ways: some translations, following an older tradition, take a word in the phrase as a place name, 'prince of Rosh, Meshech, and Tubal'; most modern translations, including the BSB, read it as a title, 'chief prince.' Either way, the text does not hand readers a definite modern national identity.
Hamon-gog means 'the horde, or multitude, of Gog'; the wordplay names the very burial ground after the vast army it swallows up, a small but pointed irony built into the Hebrew itself.
The city named 'Hamonah' shares the same root as Hamon-gog, horde or multitude; even the town's name becomes a lasting monument to the defeated multitude.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session via the makor scripture toolkit for Ezekiel 38:1-39:29. Em dashes in verses 38:4, 38:6, 38:11, 38:15, and 39:18 replaced with commas; a stray space before the closing parenthesis in 39:16 tidied. No em or en dashes used. No sermon from the trusted cluster (Begg, Keller, Piper, Ferguson, Carson) was found addressing this passage directly this session, so sermon status is none.
